source_title: NASA-UAP-D5, Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science, 1973
description_blurb:
Apollo 17 was the ninth crewed U.S. mission to the Moon, and the sixth to land Astronauts on the lunar surface. This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science on January 8, 1973, in which Dick Henry, co-investigator on the ultraviolet experiment on Apollo 17, discusses seeing results that were unexpected. 
•	Pages 119-120. “One of the most exciting results of X-ray astronomy was the fact that an X-ray background was observed over the sky that nobody had expected, and part of this is the gamma-ray background that Dr. Trombka talked about. In the UV, nobody knows, but you never know until you look. You do have to deal with this background of stars that we know is there. So, we did look at a large number of different points at high galactic latitudes, both north and south. The spectrum that we see is above this dark count. In other words, this abnormally high dark current did not, in fact, interfere with that experiment. The spectrum that we see looks like the spectrum of the hot star; however, we know that there were no hot stars within our field of view. Therefore, the most conservative interpretation, I think, is that what we're seeing is light from hot stars in the galactic plane going up out of the plane and reflecting off interstellar dust. There are certain characteristics of the spectrum, though, that don't fit that theory, and it's at least possible that this is extragalactic radiation. I'm looking forward very much to the detailed computer study of this, but it's going to take a long time.”
title: NASA-UAP-D5, Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science, 1973
summary: This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science on January 8, 1973. Dick Henry, co-investigator on the ultraviolet experiment, discusses unexpected results, including an observed UV spectrum at high galactic latitudes that resembles a hot star spectrum despite no hot stars being in the field of view. This phenomenon is considered potentially extragalactic radiation, with further computer study required. The debriefing also mentions an unexpected X-ray background and gamma-ray background.
reported_object_description: X-ray background, gamma-ray background, UV spectrum resembling a hot star spectrum without hot stars in the field of view, potentially extragalactic radiation
reported_behavior: observed over the sky, spectrum characteristics not fitting known theories, potentially extragalactic
bust_reasoning: The document describes observations of cosmic background radiation and UV spectra, not a discrete physical UAP. The 'mystery' is purely scientific, concerning the origin and characteristics of these astrophysical phenomena. The document itself provides and discusses known astrophysical explanations, such as reflected starlight or extragalactic radiation, making it a very high bust score as the 'object' is a cosmic background and the explanations are within the realm of astrophysics.
full_text:
# NASA-UAP-D5, Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science, 1973 - Page 1

CB/R.A. PARKER

MSC-07632

[NASA Logo] NATIONAL AERONAUTICS AND SPACE ADMINISTRATION

*
APOLLO 17
CREW DEBRIEFING
FOR SCIENCE

JANUARY 8, 1973

PREPARED BY
SCIENCE REQUIREMENTS BRANCH
PLANETARY AND EARTH SCIENCES DIVISION

INDEXING DATA

| DATE | OPR | # | T | PGM | SUBJECT | SIGNATOR | LOC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1-8-73 | MSC | MSC-07632 | B | APO | * | NS | 080-44F |

[NASA Graphic]

MANNED SPACECRAFT CENTER
HOUSTON, TEXAS

HS1-481238



# NASA-UAP-D5, Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science, 1973 - Page 2

119

HENRY
(CONT'D)    gravitationally holding it together. We though it might be
            in the form of ionized hydrogen. We looked for Lyman-alpha
            radiation, red shifted from the ionized hydrogen, and we
            didn't see any. We set a lower limit, which certainly ex-
            cludes the possibility that the Coma cluster is held together
            by this ionized hydrogen. I think that may leave a real
            mystery as to what is holding the thing together.

            The fourth point may turn out to be the most interesting
            thing of all. When you look in the Milky Way, you see a
            lot of UV coming from the stars, but the question is, what
            do you see when you look up to the North Galactic Pole or
            down to the South Galactic Pole. One of the most exciting
            results of X-ray astronomy was the fact that an X-ray back-
            ground was observed over the sky that nobody had expected,
            and part of this is the gamma-ray background that Dr. Trombka
            talked about. In the UV, nobody knows, but you never know
            until you look. You do have to deal with this background
            of stars that we know is there. So we did look at a large
            number of different points at high galactic latitudes, both
            north and south. The spectrum that we see is above this
            dark count. In other words, this abnormally high dark
            current did not, in fact, interfere with that experiment.
            The spectrum that we see looks like the spectrum of the hot



# NASA-UAP-D5, Apollo 17 Crew Debriefing for Science, 1973 - Page 3

120

HENRY
(CONT'D)

star; however, we know that there were no hot stars within our field of view. Therefore, the most conservative interpretation, I think, is that what we're seeing is light from hot stars in the galactic plane going up out of the plane and reflecting off interstellar dust. There are certain characteristics of the spectrum, though, that don't fit that theory, and it's at least possible that this is extragalactic radiation. I'm looking forward very much to the detailed computer study of this, but it's going to take a long time.

Fifth point: Lyman-alpha hydrogen radiation is a completely separate problem, and Gary Thomas at the University of Colorado and Charles Barthum [?] observed this from OGO-5. We obtained just an enormous amount of data on the Apollo that's going to straighten out this picture and clarify it considerably. This is hydrogen that is inside our solar system. It's sunlight reflecting off this. The hydrogen, Gary Thomas thinks, is hydrogen from interstellar space streaming through the solar system, and he is looking forward with great anticipation to getting detailed analyses of that.

One more thing: the spectrum of the Earth. I keep saying "we," but these were the guys that were there. We looked at the Earth from outside. A lot of people have observed
cover_up_reasoning: There is no evidence of concealment in the provided document. It appears to be a standard scientific debriefing transcript with no redactions, classification markings, or language suggesting suppression or witness intimidation.
title_zh: 美国国家航空航天局-UAP-D5，阿波罗17号乘员科学汇报，1973年
summary_zh: 本文件摘自1973年1月8日阿波罗17号乘员科学汇报。紫外线实验的共同研究员迪克·亨利讨论了意料之外的结果，其中包括在高银纬观测到的紫外光谱，该光谱类似于热星光谱，尽管视场内没有热星。这种现象被认为是潜在的河外辐射，需进一步的计算机研究。此次汇报还提及了意料之外的X射线背景和伽马射线背景。
reported_object_description_zh: X射线背景，伽马射线背景，视场内无热星但类似于热星光谱的紫外光谱，潜在的河外辐射
reported_behavior_zh: 在天空中观测到，光谱特征不符合已知理论，潜在的河外性质
official_resolution_zh: 尚未解决，待详细计算机研究
bust_reasoning_zh: 该文件描述的是对宇宙背景辐射和紫外光谱的观测，而非离散的物理UAP。该“谜团”纯属科学范畴，涉及这些天体物理现象的起源和特征。文件本身提供了并讨论了已知的天体物理学解释，例如反射星光或河外辐射，鉴于“物体”是宇宙背景且解释属于天体物理学范畴，因此其“揭穿”可能性极高。
cover_up_reasoning_zh: 所提供文件中没有隐瞒证据。它似乎是一份标准的科学汇报记录，没有删节、密级标记或暗示压制或恐吓证人的语言。
claims: An X-ray background was observed over the sky that nobody had expected.
A UV spectrum was observed at high galactic latitudes that looks like a hot star spectrum, but no hot stars were within the field of view.
The most conservative interpretation for the UV spectrum is light from hot stars in the galactic plane reflecting off interstellar dust, but certain characteristics don't fit this theory.
It is at least possible that the observed UV spectrum is extragalactic radiation.
Detailed computer study is needed to understand the UV spectrum, which will take a long time.
claims_zh: 天空中观测到无人预期的X射线背景。
在高银纬观测到类似于热星光谱的紫外光谱，但视场内没有热星。
对于紫外光谱最保守的解释是银河平面热星光线经星际尘埃反射，但某些特征不符合此理论。
观测到的紫外光谱至少有可能是河外辐射。
需进行详细的计算机研究以理解紫外光谱，这将耗时甚久。
bust_explanations: Reflected light from hot stars in the galactic plane off interstellar dust, explicitly stated as the 'most conservative interpretation' for the UV spectrum.
Extragalactic radiation, explicitly stated as a possible explanation for the UV spectrum.
Unidentified astrophysical background radiation (X-ray and gamma-ray backgrounds), which are known cosmic phenomena under scientific investigation.
bust_explanations_zh: 银河平面热星光线经星际尘埃反射，明确指出为紫外光谱的“最保守解释”。
河外辐射，明确指出为紫外光谱的一种可能解释。
未识别的天体物理背景辐射（X射线和伽马射线背景），这些是已知且正在科学研究中的宇宙现象。
